skill.name: data-structure-protocol
skill.description: Build and maintain a persistent DSP graph that maps code entities, imports, exports, and dependency reasons for agent navigation.
skill.summary: Map code structure into a persistent DSP graph for safer navigation and refactoring.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Most static backtick and weak-crypto detections are false positives from markdown examples, inline identifiers, and ordinary prose. The confirmed risk is that the skill instructs users to download an unpinned Python CLI from GitHub and run local commands, which creates supply-chain and local file modification risk.

medium_findings.title: Unpinned Remote CLI Download and Local Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill directs users to download a Python CLI from a GitHub raw URL and then run it locally. Because the URL targets a mutable branch and no checksum is provided, users may execute changed or compromised code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The download instruction and subsequent Python command examples are explicit. The risk is supply-chain exposure rather than confirmed malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Execution Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Most Ruby or shell backtick detections point to markdown inline identifiers, tables, or fenced examples. They are documentation text, not executable Ruby backtick evaluation inside this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are markdown prose, inline code formatting, or command tables. No Ruby source file or executable backtick expression is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak-cryptography hits occur in prose and DSP examples, not cryptographic code. No evidence found of hashing, encryption, signature verification, or insecure crypto API usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found documentation about graph entities and descriptions only. The cited lines do not implement or recommend weak cryptographic algorithms.

low_findings.title: Reference URLs Are Mostly Documentation Links
low_findings.description: Several hardcoded URLs are source metadata or reference links. They do not create background network behavior, although the CLI download URL is handled as a separate medium finding.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are static markdown links and frontmatter metadata. They are not automatic network requests or data exfiltration paths.

dangerous_patterns.title: Mutable Branch Download Before Tool Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup workflow downloads a CLI from a raw GitHub URL without pinning a commit or verifying a checksum. Users are then expected to run the downloaded Python tool against their project.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines directly describe downloading the CLI and the command pattern used to run it. No checksum or pinned revision is documented.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Project Graph Mutation Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow includes commands that create, move, and remove DSP entities in the project. This is expected behavior, but it means the skill can cause local repository changes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly list mutation-oriented CLI commands. The behavior is legitimate for the skill, so confidence reflects risk impact rather than maliciousness.

content.value_statement: Large repositories are hard for AI agents to navigate without wasting context. This skill guides agents to build a persistent DSP graph for dependencies, public APIs, and impact analysis.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use DSP for repository mapping and dependency navigation.
Defines DSP entities, stable UIDs, imports, shared exports, and reverse indexes.
Guides setup and use of the external dsp-cli.py command-line tool.
Provides workflows for creating, updating, deleting, searching, and diagnosing graph entries.
Describes how to update DSP after new files, imports, exports, moves, and deletions.
Supports impact analysis by showing import recipients and dependency paths.
limitations: Requires a separate Python CLI that may need to be downloaded from GitHub.
Graph accuracy depends on agents keeping DSP entries current after code changes.
Does not analyze internals of third-party dependencies or standard libraries.
Stores structural metadata in a project .dsp directory, which adds review overhead.

use_cases.title: Onboard to a Large Repository
use_cases.description: Use DSP to find important modules, public APIs, and dependency reasons before changing code.

use_cases.title: Plan a Refactor
use_cases.description: Trace import recipients and dependency paths before replacing a module or library.

use_cases.title: Maintain Agent Context
use_cases.description: Keep a persistent structural map so AI agents can retrieve targeted context instead of rereading the repository.

prompt_templates.title: Start a DSP Map
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a repository without DSP metadata.
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up DSP for this repository. Identify root entrypoints, create initial objects, and explain each recorded dependency reason.

prompt_templates.title: Find Refactor Impact
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate use before changing a module.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the DSP graph to find every entity affected by changing this module. Summarize direct recipients and likely test areas.

prompt_templates.title: Update DSP After Changes
prompt_templates.scenario: Keep metadata current after code edits.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my latest code changes and update DSP entries for new files, moved files, imports, exports, and changed purposes.

prompt_templates.title: Audit DSP Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit DSP coverage against current imports. Find missing objects, stale entries, orphaned nodes, cycles, and incomplete dependency reasons.

output_examples.input: Map a new service module with two exported handlers and three imports.
output_examples.output: The agent reports the new object, two function entities, shared exports, and short dependency reasons for each imported module.

output_examples.input: Find the impact of replacing an authentication package.
output_examples.output: The agent lists affected modules, why each imports the package, and a suggested order for the replacement work.

output_examples.input: Check whether DSP is current after a file move.
output_examples.output: The agent identifies the moved entity, keeps its UID stable, and updates source paths without changing unrelated graph entries.

best_practices: Review downloaded CLI code or pin it to a trusted commit before running it.
Record clear dependency reasons whenever imports or exports are added.
Review .dsp changes with the same care as source code changes.
anti_patterns: Do not update DSP for internal implementation changes that do not affect purpose or dependencies.
Do not create DSP entities for local variables or private helper details.
Do not change stable UIDs when files are renamed or moved.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps agents map code structure, dependencies, public APIs, and impact paths in a persistent DSP graph.

faq.question: Does it include the DSP CLI?
faq.answer: No. The skill instructs users to download a separate Python CLI from GitHub when it is missing.

faq.question: Does it modify project files?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented CLI workflow creates and updates files under the project .dsp directory.

faq.question: Can it analyze third-party package internals?
faq.answer: No. External dependencies are recorded as external objects, but their internals are not analyzed.

faq.question: Why are stable UIDs important?
faq.answer: Stable UIDs let DSP preserve identity when source files are renamed, moved, or reformatted.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a warning about unpinned remote CLI downloads and local command execution.
